nativeRange.habitat: Surge-swept reef fronts and submarine terraces, typically perched among branches of live stony corals (Pocillopora, Stylophora, Acropora).

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and ledges for perching
branching stony coral (Pocillopora, Stylophora, Acropora) where reef-keeping allows

diet.notes: Generally accepts a variety of meaty marine foods including mysis shrimp, frozen marine preparations, chopped shrimp/fish, and quality pellets. Has been reported to readily take prepared foods soon after acclimation.

avoidWith: ornamental shrimp (cleaner, peppermint, sexy, anemone shrimp)
conspecifics in anything but a very large system

summary: Vivid red hawkfish that perches on rockwork and branching corals in the reef aquarium. Generally considered reef-safe with corals, though it is an active predator of ornamental shrimp and small hermit crabs.
careNotes:
The Flame Hawkfish is one of the most popular small hawkfishes in the marine aquarium hobby, prized for its bright cherry-red body and contrasting black markings along the dorsal fin. It is generally regarded as hardy and adaptable for a marine fish, which contributes to its beginner-friendly care rating.

Like other hawkfishes, it lacks a swim bladder (the gas-filled organ most fish use to stay buoyant) and spends most of its time perched on rockwork or in the branches of live stony corals — particularly Pocillopora, Stylophora, and Acropora — waiting for small prey to pass below. A structured aquascape with elevated perches and branching coral or coral-skeleton equivalents is strongly recommended; in a bare or low-structure tank it has been reported to look stressed and remain unsettled.

The Flame Hawkfish is generally considered reef-safe with corals, but it is an opportunistic micro-carnivore that has been documented preying on ornamental shrimp (including cleaner, peppermint, sexy, and anemone shrimp) and small hermit crabs. Keepers who want to maintain a cleanup crew that includes ornamental shrimp should generally avoid this species.

Toward other fish it tends to be tolerant of larger, faster-moving tankmates while sometimes harassing small bottom-dwellers such as tiny gobies and dartfish. It may also be aggressive toward other hawkfishes. It is best kept singly unless the system is very large and a confirmed mated pair is introduced together.
breedingNotes: Pelagic spawner; not commonly bred in home aquaria. Captive spawning events have been reported only in very large, well-established systems with established pairs, and rearing of the planktonic larvae is generally regarded as difficult and not routinely accomplished in the hobby.
